meta.description: Synthetic C# .NET service fixture for codebase analysis benchmark

issues.description: MessageRoutes.cs returns cursor-paginated results using `nextCursor` field. But TaskRepository (via TaskRoutes) consumes messages using offset-based pagination with `pageNumber` and `pageSize`. The cursor is never read by the consumer, creating a pagination contract mismatch. TaskRoutes.GetTaskMessagesAsync calls TaskRepository.GetTaskMessagesAsync which calls MessageRepository.GetByTaskIdAsync — all using pageNumber/pageSize — while the primary message listing endpoint returns cursor-based results.
expected_evidence: MessageRoutes.cs returns `nextCursor` in response but no consumer reads it
TaskRepository.GetTaskMessagesAsync delegates to MessageRepository.GetByTaskIdAsync with pageNumber/pageSize
MessageRepository.GetByTaskIdAsync uses OFFSET/LIMIT (offset-based pagination)
TaskRoutes.cs /api/tasks/{id}/messages endpoint uses pageNumber/pageSize params
acceptable_diagnoses: Cursor pagination and offset pagination are mixed; cursor is produced but not consumed
Message list endpoint returns nextCursor but task messages use offset pagination
Inconsistent pagination strategy between message and task-message endpoints
good_fix_properties: Unify pagination strategy: either both cursor-based or both offset-based
OR: TaskRepository reads nextCursor from MessageRepository and passes it back as query param
OR: MessageRepository.GetByTaskIdAsync is replaced with cursor-based variant
issues.adjusted_behavior: Both endpoints use cursor-based pagination. TaskRoutes reads `nextCursor` from MessageRepository responses and surfaces it.

issues.description: WorkerLifecycleService.ReleaseWorkerAsync releases the pool assignment (sets member to Available) BEFORE waiting for the completion packet to be durably written. Another run can pick up the same assignment before the completion is visible, causing duplicate work or state corruption. See `ReleaseWorkerAsync`: Step 1 releases, Step 2 writes completion packet.
expected_evidence: ReleaseWorkerAsync calls ReleaseAssignmentAsync and SetMemberStatusAsync (Step 1) BEFORE WriteCompletionPacketAsync (Step 2)
Pool member becomes Available before completion is durably stored
No atomicity or ordering guarantee between release and completion write
acceptable_diagnoses: Worker release happens before completion write
Assignment released before completion packet is durably stored
Member is returned to pool before completion is acked
good_fix_properties: Write/confirm completion packet BEFORE releasing assignment
Or use a two-phase commit / transactional outbox pattern
Ensure completion is flushed to durable storage before member status change
issues.adjusted_behavior: WriteCompletionPacketAsync is called first and awaited, then ReleaseAssignmentAsync releases the assignment.

issues.description: ReviewWorkflowService caches the review verdict in `_verdictCache` and returns it from `GetCachedVerdict()` without checking whether the current task head still matches the head that was reviewed. If a new commit lands on the task branch, the cached 'approved' verdict is still returned. The `IsReviewStillValid` method exists but is not called by `GetCachedVerdict`.
expected_evidence: GetCachedVerdict returns cached verdict without comparing heads
IsReviewStillValid method exists but is not called from GetCachedVerdict
Cache stores (Verdict, ReviewedHead) tuple but ReviewedHead is never checked on retrieval
acceptable_diagnoses: Cached verdict does not verify current head matches reviewed head
Stale verdict returned when task branch has advanced
ReviewedHead stored but not compared on cache hit
good_fix_properties: GetCachedVerdict should accept currentHead parameter and verify it matches reviewed head
Or call IsReviewStillValid before returning cached verdict
If head mismatch, invalidate cache and return Pending
issues.adjusted_behavior: GetCachedVerdict takes currentHead, calls IsReviewStillValid, returns cached verdict only if heads match; otherwise returns Pending and invalidates cache.

issues.description: Program.cs hardcodes the LLM endpoint URL as `http://192.168.1.10:8080` when configuring the HttpClient for OpenAiCompatibleLlmClient. There is no environment variable or configuration fallback. This will break in any deployment where the LLM host is at a different address.
expected_evidence: Hardcoded IP address `192.168.1.10` in Program.cs
HttpClient BaseAddress set to hardcoded URI
No config/env read for the LLM endpoint URL
acceptable_diagnoses: LLM endpoint URL is hardcoded as http://192.168.1.10:8080
No configuration or environment variable fallback for LLM base URL
Hardcoded IP address for LLM service endpoint
good_fix_properties: Read LLM base URL from IConfiguration with environment variable override
Use builder.Configuration.GetValue or appsettings.json
Provide fallback default but allow override
issues.adjusted_behavior: LLM endpoint URL is read from `Configuration["Llm:BaseUrl"]` with env var `LLM__BASE_URL` override, defaulting to http://localhost:8080.

issues.description: DispatchRepository.RunBackgroundLoopAsync runs a fire-and-forget async Task.Run loop. The outer while(true) iteration does NOT have a try/catch — a single unhandled exception in DequeueBatchAsync or the ProcessEntryAsync call will crash the entire background task silently. There is no per-iteration error handling, no health signal, and no exponential backoff on failure.
expected_evidence: RunBackgroundLoopAsync uses Task.Run with while (!token.IsCancellationRequested)
No try/catch around the DequeueBatchAsync and loop body
Single exception kills the entire background task
No health check endpoint reports dispatch loop status
acceptable_diagnoses: Background dispatch loop has no per-iteration exception handling
Task.Run loop can die silently from a single unhandled exception
No try/catch around the main dispatch loop iteration
good_fix_properties: Wrap the while loop body in a try/catch
Log exceptions with context (entry ID, batch index)
Add exponential backoff between retries after failures
Add health check signal for loop status
issues.adjusted_behavior: The while loop body is wrapped in try/catch. On exception, error is logged with context, and the loop applies an exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s...) before retrying.

issues.description: ProjectTask.cs (core domain model, src/DenCore/Models/) has a field `McpToolProfile` which is an MCP-specific concept leaking into the core domain. The XML doc on the field acknowledges this is a layering violation ('This field is a layering violation — it exposes MCP concepts in the core domain') but the field remains. Core domain models should not reference MCP or tool profile concepts.
expected_evidence: ProjectTask has a field named `McpToolProfile` of type string
XML doc comment acknowledges the layering violation
Field is marked [Obsolete] but still present in the core model
acceptable_diagnoses: Core domain model ProjectTask contains MCP-specific field McpToolProfile
MCP concept leaks into core domain layer
Domain model references tool profile concept
good_fix_properties: Remove McpToolProfile field from ProjectTask
Move profile hinting to a separate mapping layer or metadata table
Use McpToolProfileRegistry for profile resolution instead
issues.adjusted_behavior: McpToolProfile field is removed from ProjectTask. Profile-to-task mapping is handled in the service layer via McpToolProfileRegistry.

issues.description: Program.cs has a /health endpoint defined as `app.MapGet("/health", () => Results.Ok(...))` that always returns HTTP 200 with status 'OK' regardless of whether the database is reachable or migrations are current. It does not probe the database connection. In a deployment scenario, load balancers and orchestrators rely on health checks to detect service degradation, and this check provides no signal of actual readiness.
expected_evidence: /health endpoint returns Results.Ok unconditionally
No database connectivity check performed
No migration status verification
Returns 200 even when database is unreachable
acceptable_diagnoses: Health check always returns OK regardless of database state
/health endpoint does not probe database connectivity
No readiness check — returns 200 even if DB is down
good_fix_properties: Execute a lightweight query (SELECT 1) against the database
Return 503 Service Unavailable if DB is unreachable
Include dependency status in health response body
issues.adjusted_behavior: /health endpoint opens a test connection and executes SELECT 1. Returns 200 with healthy status on success, 503 with degraded status on failure.

issues.description: CompletionTools.cs has MCP tool handlers where the documented tool schema field names do not match the actual route handler parameter names. The 'triage_complete' tool description documents 'projectId' as required, but the handler expects 'project_id'. The 'generate_summary' tool documents 'taskId' but the handler expects 'task_id'. Agents sending well-formed tool calls with documented field names will get binding failures.
expected_evidence: 'triage_complete' handler expects `project_id` param but tool schema documents `projectId`
'generate_summary' handler expects `task_id` param but tool schema documents `taskId`
Parameter naming convention mismatch (camelCase in docs vs snake_case in implementation)
XML doc comment on class acknowledges the mismatch
acceptable_diagnoses: Tool parameter names differ between documentation and handler implementation
Field name mismatch between tool description and route handler
MCP tool schema documents camelCase but handler expects snake_case parameters
good_fix_properties: Align parameter names between tool descriptions and route handlers
Use consistent naming convention (either all camelCase or all snake_case)
Update tool schemas to match actual handler parameter names
issues.adjusted_behavior: Both tool descriptions and route handlers use consistent parameter naming (e.g., all snake_case: project_id, run_id, result_json, task_id, output_format).

issues.description: TaskRepository uses a `static readonly ConcurrentDictionary<long, ProjectTask>` as an in-memory cache. While ConcurrentDictionary provides per-operation thread safety, the cache seeding logic (`SeedCacheAsync`) and invalidation pattern (`Invalidate` then immediate re-check) can race under concurrent requests. The comment on the cache field notes 'Not thread-safe — concurrent writes can cause duplicate entries or stale reads'. Under load, cache entries can be lost or throw.
expected_evidence: Static ConcurrentDictionary cache field with 'not thread-safe' comment
SeedCacheAsync populates from DB with no lock against concurrent reads/writes
InsertAsync and GetByIdAsync both write to cache without synchronization
Comment explicitly warns about thread safety issues
acceptable_diagnoses: In-memory cache access is not properly synchronized
ConcurrentDictionary used but seeding and invalidation race
Cache access pattern is not thread-safe despite ConcurrentDictionary
good_fix_properties: Use proper synchronization (lock or SemaphoreSlim) around cache operations
Use Lazy<T> or LazyInitializer for thread-safe seeding
Replace with IDistributedCache for multi-instance safety
issues.adjusted_behavior: Cache seeding uses a SemaphoreSlim to prevent concurrent initialization. Cache read/write operations are synchronized correctly.

issues.description: docs/api-contract.md documents the document-by-ID endpoint as `GET /api/documents/{id}` but the actual route in DocumentRoutes.cs is mapped to `GET /api/documents/by-id/{id}`. A client following the API documentation will get a 404. The docs say `/api/documents/{id}` but code says `/api/documents/by-id/{id}`.
expected_evidence: api-contract.md lists `GET /api/documents/{id}`
DocumentRoutes.cs maps `GET /api/documents/by-id/{id}`
Path mismatch between documentation and implementation
No route for /api/documents/{id} exists
acceptable_diagnoses: Documented API path does not match actual route
Docs say /api/documents/{id} but code uses /api/documents/by-id/{id}
Path mismatch between API contract and DocumentRoutes
good_fix_properties: Update api-contract.md to reflect actual route path /api/documents/by-id/{id}
Or add a redirect from /api/documents/{id} to /api/documents/by-id/{id}
issues.adjusted_behavior: api-contract.md lists `GET /api/documents/by-id/{id}` matching the route implementation.

issues.description: DocumentRoutes.cs `by-id/{id}` endpoint calls `DocumentRepository.GetByIdAsync` which returns null when a document is not found. However, the route passes the (possibly null) result directly to `Results.Ok(doc)` instead of checking for null and returning `Results.NotFound()`. This means missing documents return HTTP 200 with an empty/null body instead of the correct HTTP 404.
expected_evidence: DocumentRoutes.cs by-id handler: `return Results.Ok(doc)` — no null check
DocumentRepository.GetByIdAsync returns null for missing docs
Missing document returns 200 OK with null body instead of 404
The by-slug endpoint correctly handles null (returns 404)
acceptable_diagnoses: GetByIdAsync returns null but route does not convert to 404
Document by-id endpoint returns Ok(null) instead of NotFound()
No 404 handling for missing document in by-id route
good_fix_properties: Check if doc is null before returning
Return Results.NotFound() when document is not found
Consistent 404 handling with by-slug endpoint
issues.adjusted_behavior: by-id handler checks for null and returns Results.NotFound() instead of Results.Ok(null).

issues.description: WorkerPoolRepository.ReleaseAssignmentAsync updates the assignment state unconditionally with `UPDATE worker_assignments SET state = 'completed' WHERE id = @Id`. There is no check for whether the assignment was already released (state = 'completed'). Re-releasing an already-released assignment produces a nonsensical state transition (completed -> completed) and can cause duplicate processing in downstream consumers that watch for state changes.
expected_evidence: ReleaseAssignmentAsync does not check current state before updating
No WHERE clause checking state != 'completed'
Already-released assignments can be 're-released'
No idempotency check or release nonce verification
acceptable_diagnoses: ReleaseAssignmentAsync can succeed on already-released assignments
No idempotency check in release operation
State transitions not validated — completed -> completed is allowed
good_fix_properties: Check current state before updating (WHERE state != 'completed')
Return NoOp/count=0 when already released
Use release nonce for idempotent release operations
issues.adjusted_behavior: ReleaseAssignmentAsync adds `AND state != 'completed'` to the WHERE clause. Returns a result indicating whether the release was performed or was a no-op.
